title: Accounts Merge
problem_statement: Given a list of accounts where each element accounts[i] is a list of strings, where the first element accounts[i][0] is a name, and the rest of the elements are emails representing emails of the account. Now, we would like to merge these accounts. Two accounts definitely belong to the same person if there is some common email to both accounts. Note that even if two accounts have the same name, they may belong to different people as people could have the same name. A person can have any number of accounts initially, but all of them have the same name. After merging the accounts, return the accounts in the following format: the first element of each account is the name, and the rest of the elements are emails in sorted order. The accounts themselves can be returned in any order.

examples.explanation: The first and third John's are the same person as they have "johnsmith@mail.com" in common. The second John and Mary are distinct as none of their emails are in the common with these.
All accounts are distinct in this case.

constraints: accounts[i][j] (for j > 0) is a valid email.
intuition: This is a graph connectivity problem where emails are nodes, and accounts connect emails. We need to find connected components of emails and group them under the same name.

approaches.name: Union-Find on Emails
approaches.description: Treat each email as a node. For each account, union all emails in it. Then, group emails by their root parent and sort them.
approaches.time: O(A * α(A)) where A is total emails
approaches.why_not: DFS/BFS would work but Union-Find is more efficient for this disjoint set problem.
interview_commentary: Union-Find connects emails that appear in the same account. We union each email to the first email in the account.
Path compression in find() optimizes lookups. We use TreeSet to sort emails automatically.
This handles transitive connections: if A connects B and C, and C connects D, all are merged.

approaches.name: DFS for Connected Components
approaches.description: Build a graph where emails are nodes and accounts are edges. Use DFS to find connected components of emails.
approaches.why_not: Union-Find is usually faster for this, but DFS is simpler to implement if Union-Find is unfamiliar.
interview_commentary: Build undirected graph: each account connects all its emails bidirectionally.
DFS traverses connected emails, collecting them into components.
Sort each component and add the name at the front.

interview_qa.a: Union-Find is more efficient for union operations; DFS is fine but may be slower for large inputs.

interview_qa.q: How to handle multiple accounts with same name?
interview_qa.a: Only merge if they share emails; same name alone doesn't merge.
Required by problem for consistent output order.

interview_qa.q: What if an email appears in multiple accounts?
interview_qa.a: It gets merged into one group with all connected emails.
